contentBody:
A junior developer is assigned various tasks for a new company website. They need to determine which technology (HTML, CSS, or JavaScript) is appropriate for each task.

Review the following tasks and select all that are correctly matched to their technology:
option.1: HTML â€” "Add a navigation bar with links to Home, About, and Contact pages"
option.2: CSS â€” "Make the page title red and increase its font size to 48 pixels"
option.3: JavaScript â€” "Add a paragraph of text describing the company history"
option.4: HTML â€” "Show a popup message when the user clicks the Subscribe button"
answerExplanation:
**Option A â€” Correct:** `.classname { }` is the correct CSS syntax. The `.` prefix is what tells the browser "look for elements with this value in their `class` attribute." **Option B â€” Incorrect:** Classes do NOT have a uniqueness requirement. Any number of elements on a page can share the same class value. This is the entire point â€” classes enable styling a group of elements with one rule. **Option C â€” Correct:** Classes are tag-agnostic. A class like `.highlight` can be applied to a `

`, a `

`, a ``, an `
  • `, or any other HTML element. The same CSS rule applies to all of them. **Option D â€” Correct:** This is the primary use case for class selectors â€” creating a named, reusable style that can be applied to any group of elements that should share that visual appearance. **Option E â€” Incorrect:** An element can have multiple classes. They are listed in the `class` attribute separated by spaces: `class="task task-high featured"`. Each class in the list is applied independently. **Option F â€” Correct:** In HTML, `class="class-name"` is how you assign a class to an element. The CSS selector `.class-name { }` matches elements where `class-name` is present in the `class` attribute. **Correct Answers: A, C, D, F**

# Q2: Building the HTML Structure for a Profile Card

## Problem Description

Create the HTML structure (content only, no styling) for a user profile card. This practices the HTML role: defining what elements exist on the page.

## Requirements

Create HTML that includes:
1. A heading with the user's name: `Priya Sharma`
2. A paragraph with their role: `Full Stack Developer`
3. A paragraph with their location: `Bangalore, India`
4. A paragraph with their bio: `Passionate about building accessible web applications.`
5. A button with the text: `View Portfolio`
6. A hyperlink with text `Connect on LinkedIn` pointing to `https://linkedin.com`

Note: Do not add any CSS styling. Focus only on the HTML structure.

## Expected Output

When rendered in a browser (with browser default styling), the page should show:
- A bold heading "Priya Sharma"
- "Full Stack Developer" as a paragraph
- "Bangalore, India" as a paragraph
- The bio text as a paragraph
- A clickable button labeled "View Portfolio"
- A hyperlink labeled "Connect on LinkedIn"

## Difficulty
Medium

## Category
Implementation

## Type
Coding
answerExplanation: Use `.task { }` for shared layout properties, then write separate rules for `.task-high`, `.task-medium`, and `.task-low` for the distinct border and background colors.

contentBody: A web developer says: "I need to make the navigation menu slide down smoothly when the user hovers over it." Which technology is primarily responsible for implementing this behavior?
option.1: HTML, because navigation menus are defined in HTML
option.2: CSS, because hover effects are CSS transitions
option.3: JavaScript, because smooth sliding requires programming logic
option.4: Both CSS and JavaScript can achieve this, but neither HTML
answerExplanation:
**Why A (`#`) is wrong:** The `#` prefix is used for ID selectors. `#hero { }` targets the element with `id="hero"`. The `id` must be unique; the `#` selector is its counterpart in CSS.

**Why B (`@`) is wrong:** The `@` symbol is reserved for CSS at-rules: `@media`, `@import`, `@keyframes`, `@font-face`, etc. These are not selectors â€” they are directives that configure CSS behavior.

**Why C (`.`) is correct:** The dot is the class selector prefix. `.highlight { }` targets every element with `class="highlight"` (or any element where `highlight` is one of the classes in the `class` attribute). This is the most commonly used selector type in practical CSS.

**Why D (`~`) is wrong:** The tilde (`~`) is used in CSS as the general sibling combinator, which selects elements that share the same parent and follow a specified element. It is a combinator, not a prefix for attribute-based selectors.

**Memory tip:** Class = `.` Think of "dot com" â€” `.com` is a domain. A class is like a category or domain that many elements can belong to.

# Q2: Build a Simple Login Form

## Problem Description

Build the HTML structure for a login form without using a `<form>` wrapper.

## Requirements

Create the following input elements with appropriate attributes:

1. An `<h2>` heading: `Sign in to Your Account`
2. An email input with:
   - `type="email"`, `name="email"`, `id="login-email"`, `placeholder="Email address"`, `required`
3. A password input with:
   - `type="password"`, `name="password"`, `id="login-password"`, `placeholder="Password"`, `required`
4. A checkbox input for "Remember me":
   - `type="checkbox"`, `name="remember"`, `id="remember-me"`
   - A `<label>` for the checkbox with `for="remember-me"` and text: `Remember me on this device`
5. A submit button:
   - `type="submit"`, `class="btn btn-login"`, button text: `Sign In`
6. An `<a>` link below the button:
   - `href="forgot-password.html"`, link text: `Forgot your password?`

## Expected Output

A login form with email field, password field, "remember me" checkbox with label, a sign-in button, and a forgot password link.

Medium

Implementation

## Type
Coding

option.3: All five `<a>` elements on the page will be styled purple with an underline.
option.4: The link in `<footer>` will also be styled because the tag selector applies regardless of where the element is nested.
answerExplanation:
**Option A â€” Correct:** `.classname { }` is the correct CSS syntax. The `.` prefix is what tells the browser "look for elements with this value in their `class` attribute." **Option B â€” Incorrect:** Classes do NOT have a uniqueness requirement. Any number of elements on a page can share the same class value. This is the entire point â€” classes enable styling a group of elements with one rule. **Option C â€” Correct:** Classes are tag-agnostic. A class like `.highlight` can be applied to a `

`, a `

`, a ``, an `
  • `, or any other HTML element. The same CSS rule applies to all of them. **Option D â€” Correct:** This is the primary use case for class selectors â€” creating a named, reusable style that can be applied to any group of elements that should share that visual appearance. **Option E â€” Incorrect:** An element can have multiple classes. They are listed in the `class` attribute separated by spaces: `class="task task-high featured"`. Each class in the list is applied independently. **Option F â€” Correct:** In HTML, `class="class-name"` is how you assign a class to an element. The CSS selector `.class-name { }` matches elements where `class-name` is present in the `class` attribute. **Correct Answers: A, C, D, F**

contentBody: What does a CSS tag selector use to identify which elements to style?
option.4: The element's position in the page (first, second, third)
answerExplanation:
**Why A (`#`) is wrong:** The `#` prefix is used for ID selectors. `#hero { }` targets the element with `id="hero"`. The `id` must be unique; the `#` selector is its counterpart in CSS.

**Why B (`@`) is wrong:** The `@` symbol is reserved for CSS at-rules: `@media`, `@import`, `@keyframes`, `@font-face`, etc. These are not selectors â€” they are directives that configure CSS behavior.

**Why C (`.`) is correct:** The dot is the class selector prefix. `.highlight { }` targets every element with `class="highlight"` (or any element where `highlight` is one of the classes in the `class` attribute). This is the most commonly used selector type in practical CSS.

**Why D (`~`) is wrong:** The tilde (`~`) is used in CSS as the general sibling combinator, which selects elements that share the same parent and follow a specified element. It is a combinator, not a prefix for attribute-based selectors.

**Memory tip:** Class = `.` Think of "dot com" â€” `.com` is a domain. A class is like a category or domain that many elements can belong to.

# Q2 â€“ Implementation Coding (Medium Difficulty)

## Question

Build a task list application UI using class selectors to differentiate tasks by priority level.

**Requirements:**

Create an HTML page with a list of tasks. Each `<li>` element represents a task. Assign classes to indicate priority:
- `class="task-high"` â€” red left border, light red background
- `class="task-medium"` â€” orange left border, light orange background
- `class="task-low"` â€” green left border, light green background

**Base styles for all tasks (use a `.task` class on every `<li>`):**
- `padding: 12px 16px`
- `margin-bottom: 8px`
- `border-radius: 4px`
- `font-size: 15px`
- `list-style: none`

**Priority styles:**
- `.task-high`: `border-left: 4px solid crimson; background-color: #fde8e8;`
- `.task-medium`: `border-left: 4px solid darkorange; background-color: #fdf3e8;`
- `.task-low`: `border-left: 4px solid green; background-color: #e8fde8;`

**Sample tasks:**
- "Fix critical login bug" (high)
- "Update documentation" (low)
- "Review pull request" (medium)
- "Patch security vulnerability" (high)
- "Write unit tests" (medium)

**Difficulty:** Medium
**Type:** Coding
**Category:** Implementation
answerExplanation: Use `.task { }` for shared layout properties, then write separate rules for `.task-high`, `.task-medium`, and `.task-low` for the distinct border and background colors.
